I think all of the `appAuth` settings should use `['appAuth'].get('publicKeyID') or None` instead of `['appAuth']['publicKeyID']`. Three reasons for that:

1) If they forget to enter the correct values, the constructor will treat it as if the user didn't pass anything, instead of 0-length data (since the developer console defaults to showing these as empty strings).
2) In particular, it might be the case (though I'm not sure) that cryptography would treat an empty string passphrase differently from `None`.
3) This would allow the user to skip having to define `privateKey`, if they instead wanted to pass a system path (`rsa_private_key_file_sys_path`) via the keyword arguments I suggested.

Not sure it's needed for all modules. When a logger is created, the default level is ``NOTSET`` that means everything is logged if the logger is the root logger. If the logger is not the root logger (i.e. ``ddtrace.tracer`` module), then it should search the level of the ancestor (``ddtrace``) and so on.

If all loggers have the same logging level, it's enough to set the level of the root logger:
```python
# in the ddtrace/__init__.py
log = logging.getLogger('ddtrace')  # you can use the __name__ it's just to be clear
log.setLevel(logging.WARNING)
```

Reference: https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging.Logger.setLevel

It's a fixture, you can request a pre-built instance by just adding `monkeypatch` to the test signature, and then do `monkeypatch.setattr(...)`.

Personally I'd probably just use `from unittest.mock import patch` and then `with patch.object(...)` instead, since that's what we use elsewhere in the tests. But I don't feel strongly, using the `monkeypatch` fixture is OK too.

There are edge cases where `isfile` and `isdir` can both be false, such as [device files](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Device_file). While it doesn’t really matter practically, it is semantically more correct to check for “not directory” because those special files are still readable. It also matches the directory check in `is_venv_in_project`, which makes the code easier to reason with.

I’m wondering though, it may be a better idea to merge the two checks. `is_venv_in_project` already checks for directory, so when you reach this part, an `exists` check is enough. We shouldn’t reach here if it’s a directory; if it exists, we want to try reading it (and fails with a straight-up error if that fails).

Will this work on Windows, where getpreferredencoding could well be something like CP850, but Python could be installed in a directory with Unicode characters? Especially in Python 2.5, where per-user installs could be under the user's home directory (and so will be more likely to contain non-ASCII characters). I'd suggest encoding the JSON data in such a way that it is pure-ASCII before sending it to the caller, and then you can decode it back to Unicode on the receiving end.

Luckily I re-factored the unit tests, because it made me realize that this could fail, because the generator still has to do:

```python
if request == _helper_threads.STOP:
    logger.debug()
    break
```

after the blocking `get()` completes, so this call to `close()` might happen **before** the generator gets those instructions.

I'm inclined to say we should either:

- Drop this statement completely (we are "safe" since we know that `STOP` has been pulled off the queue)
- Do a `while True` loop that tries to `close()` and `break`-s if it succeeds
- Do a `while request_generator.gi_running: pass` loop to block until it stops running and then call `close()`

@phobologic Hi Michael, thanks for you interest in this new DAG implementation. As I've stated in f6733af3, the previous implementation is flawed in that it cannot handle some of the corner cases.

Take a real world example here, software A depends on B and D, while B depends on C and C depends on D. What this `transitive_reduction` does is to simply the dependency tree to `A -> B -> C -> D`, while the previous implementation cannot return the correct result.

As for the implemetation, the idea is with all current edges(`A -> B`, `A -> D`, `B -> C`, `C -> D`), we will join the edges to create a new edge if the end of one edge is the beginning of another, so for `A -> B` and `B -> C`, we'll create `A -> C`, and, since we are running in a loop, we'll use `A -> C` and `C -> D` to create `A -> D` and all that. After this loop, we have marked all the connectivity between nodes. Then we'll mark all those newly constructed edges and remove the unnecessary nodes.

I've discovered this flaw during a Python 3.5 test run, where the key order of dictionaries are different from Python 2.7 and Python 3.6.

As for performance, correct me if I'm wrong but I think both implementation are O(N^3), and I don't believe this could be an issue since we are using DAG to handle Cloudformation templates, which will never be huge.

A mixin seems appropriate for that -- but having the mixin handle the environment and everything seems way overboard. I've changed this to [an extremely simple mixin named `ExecutablePathProvider`](https://github.com/cosmicexplorer/pants/blob/0b1c385cba373b3da4ce2a431f1868c6ede30086/src/python/pants/binaries/binary_tool.py#L146) instead, and consumers such as BuildLocalPythonDistributions can [do any environment modifications themselves](https://github.com/cosmicexplorer/pants/blob/0b1c385cba373b3da4ce2a431f1868c6ede30086/src/python/pants/backend/python/tasks/build_local_python_distributions.py#L116) (I've also [moved the path modification utility method to contextutil](https://github.com/cosmicexplorer/pants/blob/0b1c385cba373b3da4ce2a431f1868c6ede30086/src/python/pants/util/contextutil.py#L30)). @gsalgado this is the mechanism that now allows us to do avoid doing heavy computations on response data both during validation and processing.  Whatever *pre-processing* that *must* be done on response data in order to validate it is now required to be done before validation.  Each `Manager` class will then implement the pre-processing step via a `_normalize_response` method, and the data returned is in whatever format is deemed appropriate for the given response type.

Here, for `NodeData` responses, the returned format is a `Tuple[Tuple[Hash32, bytes], ...]`, aka a tuple of `(keccak(node), node)` pairs.  For `Receipts` I think this is going to be a 3-tuple of `[receipts, receipts_root, trie_kv_data]`, ie. the receipts paired up with the return of `mk_trie_nodes_and_data`.  Happy to hear feedback on this as it's the best I could come up with.

Yep, to reiterate some stuff we talked about for the record here...

Originally, all the databroker tests inserted documents manually like this. It was verbose and hard to maintain, and sometimes didn't quite match up with the actual documents generated by bluesky as things changed. Therefore, we decided to use the RunEngine to generate all documents used in databroker tests. We use as a `RE` figure to do that.

A downside of this is that databroker still supports py2, while bluesky only support py3, so tests that use the `RE` fixture cannot be tested on py2. Such tests as decorated with the `@py3` decorator so that the py2 tests skip them. This obviously hurts on py2 test coverage. One possible solution is to generate all the document in Python 3, stash them somewhere online as JSON, and download them for use in the py2 tests. We hope to get around to doing that some day and boosting our py2 test coverage.

The errors from joiner callback are
```
/**
 * This function pointer is called to notify the completion of a join operation.
 *
 * @param[in]  aError    OT_ERROR_NONE if the join process succeeded.
 *                       OT_ERROR_SECURITY if the join process failed due to security credentials.
 *                       OT_ERROR_NOT_FOUND if no joinable network was discovered.
 *                       OT_ERROR_RESPONSE_TIMEOUT if a response timed out.
 * @param[in]  aContext  A pointer to application-specific context.
 *
 */
```

We already have `SPINEL_STATUS_JOIN_SECURITY` and `SPINEL_STATUS_JOIN_NO_PEERS` which map nicely to two of the error cases above.

There's really no way to stop channels from putting format commands in the titles of their streams, unless you want to look for them and strip them before LazyFormatter puts them into PlayerOutput's self.title. The format codes are harmless and can't execute naughty code, it could just cause a desync of what is displayed in webbrowser at twitch.tv and in streamlink's window title. Because there's no motivation for evil hackers to insert format codes, and because streamers must put the substring "\$" in their title to activate them, it's more likely a "feature" than a "bug" that streamers could put player specific format codes in their stream title.

The idea of `_mpv_title_escape' is to render multiple `$` in a row correctly, to reduce accidentally triggering format codes, and to enable triggering format codes when desired. VLC can do it in a one-liner, but mpv has that annoying "disable format codes from this point on" token.

I think the best practice for sympl is "import sympl as sp", then you don't need to go back and import each wrapper individually. I think if you do that, the effort/mental load/amount of code needed/etc. are equivalent between the two options (wrapping by accessing an Object, or by accessing a method). In that case, the main difference between the two is that one results in a more complex API with redundancies, breaking a Python idiom/best practice.

And I think no matter which way we do it, "wrapping an object" is a non-model-specific goal that should be fully implemented in Sympl. If it is necessary to add these methods to a subclass of Prognostic in CliMT, then that's saying something is wrong/insufficient in Sympl.

Hi Zeb, 

On your points: Yes, yes and yes. I'll deal with the `F2x` business here mostly. The other stuff I'll promise I'll implement in the future!

The treatment of `F2x` is inconsistent at the moment. `F2x` and `tcrecs` (both specifiable by the user) are used to generate the `q` coefficients that govern the temperature change at each forcing time step. The forcing routine calculates the CO2 forcing which then feeds back into the temperature calculation (through F). 

It sounds counter-intuitive, but with the way the model interacts with `F2x` and ECS, then the lower the value of `F2x` the greater the temperature change if net non-CO2 forcing is positive. If you have a low value of `F2x` then it takes less forcing to generate the same temperature change, so anything on top of this will be more effective than under a high `F2x`.

If we allow the user to specify `F2x`, then either this makes the forcing scale factor for CO2 redundant (the first element in the `scale` option; probably a desirable outcome) or there is an inconsistency (in that the user-specified value may not agree with the number coming out of the forcing routine). CO2 forcing uncertainty comes from the carbon cycle, so I don't think we need to implement a further scaling factor on top of this. In that case I think I should adjust the forcing routine to ensure that it matches the user-specified `F2x`. This was done in v1.0 with the Myhre forcing and in v1.1 with Etminan (but incorrectly).

The Ghan method slows down the code slightly. Running just the `aerocom` method should be comparable to the old regression-based treatment as they are both linear relationships.
